Novo Nordisk Receives FDA Approval for Weekly Insulin
data.description:
Danish pharmaceutical giant Novo Nordisk has secured FDA approval in the United States for Awiqli, the first long-acting basal insulin administered once weekly for adults with type 2 diabetes. The commercial launch is anticipated during the second half of 2026.

The approval is based on Phase 3 clinical trials involving approximately 2,680 patients, where the treatment demonstrated reduced blood sugar levels and a safety profile consistent with daily insulin regimens.

data.title: Spot gold extends gains, rises nearly 2% to $4,465.79/oz
Hanx Biopharmaceuticals' 2025 Loss Widens
For hungry markets, it was a kids' menu TACO
data.description:
A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Stella Qiu

Investors betting on TACO seemed to get what they wanted. After Nasdaq officially slumped into correction territory overnight, President Donald Trump announced he was delaying his planned attacks on Iranian power stations by another 10 days.

Yet, the price reaction has not been as totally awe inspiring as he would have hoped for. Brent crude futures slipped less than 1% to $107.24 a barrel, hardly unwinding an almost 6% surge overnight. Wall Street futures have bounced by 0.4%, but that pales in comparison with the rally on Tuesday when Trump first delayed his initial 48-hour deadline to five days.

Europe's EUROSTOXX 50 futures rose 0.5%, while Treasuries and the dollar are mostly flat.

It could be investors are growing numb to Trump's verbal reassurances. Many reckoned by extending the deadline twice, he is merely kicking the can down the road, which in itself suggests the four-week-old war is not ending anytime soon.

Adding to the jitters were reports that another 10,000 U.S. troops might be heading to the Middle East, fanning fears of an imminent ground conflict. There is a real risk of mission creep dragging the U.S. into a full-fledged war, yet with no certainty the Strait of Hormuz could be reopened anytime soon.

All of that made for cautious trading into the weekend. MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan is down 2.4% for the week and over 11% from its peak in late February. Japan's Nikkei was similarly down 10% from its February top. South Korea's KOSPI shed 1.5%, bringing its weekly loss to a steep 7%.

Meanwhile, central banks are warning of raising rates for the worst possible reason, fighting a 1970s-style stagflation threat. Norway's Norges Bank raised eyebrows with a spectacular U-turn on Thursday, flagging rate hikes this year after previously forecasting three cuts by 2028.

At the Fed, Governor Michael Barr and Vice Chair Philip Jeffers both sounded concerned about sticky inflation. A trio of their colleagues will get to speak later today, and markets will be listening for any more hawkish views.

Stakes will be high given the seismic shift in market pricing lately, with a rate hike in September about 50% priced in. Fed officials were projecting a rate cut this year.

Key developments that could influence markets on Friday:

-- Developments in the Middle East conflict

-- The UK publishes retail sales for February

-- Fed officials Thomas Barkin, Anna Paulson and Mary Daly speak

(Editing by Sam Holmes)

data.title: Spot silver rises nearly 3% to $70.06/oz
MORNING BID EUROPE-For hungry markets, it was a kids' menu TACO
Singapore Looks to Add Gold Sheen to Its Status as Financial Hub
data.description:
By Megan Cheah


Last year, gold was one of the best-performing asset classes, setting a string of record highs as retail and institutional investors alike flocked to the precious metal.

Despite a pullback in prices this year amid heightened market volatility, Singapore is betting that gold-trading demand will continue and it wants a bigger piece of the action.

Singapore's central bank and its precious-metals industry association are looking at ways to strengthen the city-state's gold-trading ecosystem, echoing similar moves by rival financial hub Hong Kong.

A group set up by the Monetary Authority of Singapore and the Singapore Bullion Market Association will explore new ways to meet growing interest among investors to vault and trade gold in Singapore, they said Friday.

Potential options include developing gold-related capital market products, setting internationally aligned standards for vaulting and logistics, and building a clearing system to support over-the-counter settlement for trading gold bars in Singapore.

The MAS will also look at providing vaulting services for foreign central banks and sovereign entities, it said.

The group will be co-chaired by the MAS and the bullion group, and be made up of private-sector stakeholders including lenders DBS Bank, ICBC Standard Bank, JPMorgan Chase Bank, UBS and United Overseas Bank, stock-exchange operator SGX Group, and the World Gold Council.

That follows a similar effort by Hong Kong to also boost its appeal as a gold-trading hub. The city recently signed a deal with the Shanghai Gold Exchange to foster gold trading, and is planning to expand its storage capacity for the yellow metal.

Chee Hong Tat, the MAS's deputy board chair, said there is likely room for more than one regional trading center for gold as rising uncertainty gives more investors reason to pivot to the safe-haven asset.

"I think the space is big enough for us to coexist and for both cities to be able to grow our respective services," said Chee, who is also Singapore's minister for national development. "There are some overlaps in the clients that we serve and the market segments that we target, but it's also not completely identical."

Though gold has traditionally benefited during times of uncertainty as a safe store of value, that hasn't been the case during the current period of geopolitical turbulence centered around the war in the Middle East.

The onset of the conflict changed gold's fortunes, causing a surge in energy prices that has raised alarm about an inflationary shock. That could limit room for central banks to lower rates. An environment of higher interest rates tends to tarnish the shine of the noninterest-bearing asset.

Recent gold price action shows that geopolitical tensions aren't enough to sustain the metal's rally, as safe-haven demand is largely overshadowed by higher energy prices and a stronger dollar, said ING commodities strategist Ewa Manthey in a recent note.

Still, any deeper correction in gold would likely attract buyers, particularly from central banks and longer-term investors, she added.

Singapore's move to boost its gold ecosystem isn't premised around the metal's near-term moves, Chee said, noting that building up the city-state's gold-trading capabilities would take time and close industry collaboration.

"We are not placing bets on whether the prices in the short term will go up or go down," Chee said.


Write to Megan Cheah at megan.cheah@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-27-26 0130ET

data.title: Sulphur price surge creates windfall for China's struggling oil refiners
Why so many Germans continue to shun stocks and funds
data.description:
FRANKFURT (dpa-AFX) - For the majority of people in Germany, friends and family remain the primary source of information when it comes to investing. Nearly one-third (31.2 percent) of the 3,203 adults surveyed by the Ipsos polling institute on behalf of Commerzbank seek investment tips within their private circles.

Bank and financial advisors rank closely behind in the representative survey conducted last autumn (29.7 percent). However, when asked whether they had consulted their bank or a financial advisor regarding investment products in the past twelve months, nearly 80 percent answered in the negative.

Overnight deposits over securities

Overall, Germans continue to save at a record pace: nearly three-quarters of respondents reported setting money aside either regularly (43.7 percent) or occasionally (28.4 percent). By far the most popular vehicles remain overnight deposit accounts, fixed-term deposits, and traditional savings accounts.

Approximately half of savers avoid securities such as equities, ETFs, funds, or bonds, primarily citing a self-proclaimed lack of expertise or a deep-seated aversion to risk.

Are savers leaving money on the table?

"Many people in Germany save diligently, but too conservatively," says Thomas Schaufler, Head of Private Customers at Commerzbank. "Despite high savings rates, the majority underutilize higher-yielding asset classes like securities, thereby forfeiting long-term financial potential."

Schaufler points out that approximately 3 trillion euros are currently sitting in German accounts, most of which earn no interest. "Particularly with regard to retirement planning, it is more important than ever to strategically invest liquid assets and capitalize on the opportunities offered by capital markets," the Commerzbank executive added./ben/DP/zb

Intesa finances USD290 million storage project in the US
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Intesa Sanpaolo is strengthening its US presence with a new energy deal, as reported by Il Sole 24 Ore on Friday.

Through its IMI Corporate & Investment Banking division led by Mauro Micillo, the group participated in a financing round of approximately USD290 million, alongside a syndicate of international banks, for a battery storage project in Boulder City, Nevada.

The transaction involves the Roccasecca project, a 127 MW / 506 MWh facility expected to be operational by 2026, aimed at enhancing the flexibility and efficiency of the power grid in the US Southwest, the newspaper continues.

The US remains a key market for the group led by Carlo Messina, particularly in the energy and infrastructure sectors. This deal adds to a growing track record: over the past three years, Intesa has participated in transactions totaling approximately USD50 billion in the country, spanning renewables, infrastructure, digital, and capital markets.

By Claudia Cavaliere, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to redazione@alliancenews.com

Chinese Conglomerate Citic Reports Higher 2025 Revenue
data.description:
By Sherry Qin


Chinese conglomerate Citic's 2025 revenue rose slightly as strong performances by its financial services and advanced manufacturing businesses offset the weakness of its consumption and urbanization segments.

The Beijing-based company reported a 3% increase for revenue to 769.26 billion yuan, equivalent to US$111.30 billion. Its net profit edged 0.9% higher to 58.73 billion yuan.

Its financial services segment's revenue rose 6.2% to 290.88 billion yuan, driven by growth in Citic Securities' brokerage business and fee income from Citic Bank. Its advanced materials business recorded a 3% revenue increase to 335.46 billion yuan while its manufacturing segment's revenue rose 12.5%.

The company declared a final dividend of 0.385 yuan per share for 2025, taking its annual dividend to 0.585 yuan per share, up 6.4%.


Write to Sherry Qin at sherry.qin@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-27-26 0057ET
